volumeTitle: Trojan Women. Iphigenia among the Taurians. Ion
label: Trojan Women (Section 320 - Section 560)

sections.label: Section 320
sections.translation: am making this torch to blaze and show its light , giving to you , O Hymen , giving , O Hecate , a light , at the maiden ’ s wedding , as the custom is .

sections.label: Section 325
sections.translation: Nimbly lift the foot ; lead the dance on high , with cries of joy , as if to greet my father ’ s happy fate . The dance is sacred . Come , Phoebus , now , for it is in your temple

sections.label: Section 330
sections.translation: among your bay-trees that I minister . Hail Hymen , god of marriage ! Hymen , hail ! Dance , mother , and laugh ! link your steps with me , and circle in the delightful measure , now here , now there .

sections.label: Section 335
sections.translation: Salute the bride on her wedding-day with hymns and cries of joy . Come , you maids of Phrygia in fair raiment , sing my marriage

sections.label: Section 340
sections.translation: with the husband fate ordains that I should wed .

sections.label: Section 341
sections.translation: Hold the frantic maiden , royal mistress , lest with nimble foot she rush to the Argive army .

sections.label: Section 343
sections.original: Ἥφαιστε , δᾳδουχεῖς μὲν ἐν γάμοις βροτῶν ,
sections.translation: You god of fire , it is yours to light the bridal torch for men , but piteous is the flame you kindle here ,

sections.label: Section 345
sections.translation: beyond my blackest expectation . Ah , my child ! how little did I ever dream that such would be your marriage , a captive , and of Argos too ! Give up the torch to me ; you do not bear its blaze aright in your wild frantic course , nor have your afflictions left you in your sober senses ,

sections.label: Section 350
sections.original: ἐσωφρονήκασʼ , ἀλλʼ ἔτʼ ἐν ταὐτῷ μένεις .
sections.translation: but still you are as frantic as before . Take in those torches , Trojan friends , and for her wedding madrigals weep your tears instead .

sections.label: Section 353
sections.translation: O mother , crown my head with victor ’ s wreaths ; rejoice in my royal match ; lead me

sections.label: Section 355
sections.original: καὶ πέμπε , κἂν μὴ τἀμά σοι πρόθυμά γʼ ᾖ ,
sections.translation: and if you find me unwilling at all , thrust me there by force ; for if Loxias is indeed a prophet , Agamemnon , that famous king of the Achaeans , will find in me a bride more vexatious than Helen . For I will slay him and lay waste his home

sections.label: Section 360
sections.original: ποινὰς ἀδελφῶν καὶ πατρὸς λαβοῦσʼ ἐμοῦ ⋯
sections.translation: to avenge my father ’ s and my brothers ’ death . But let that go ; I will not tell of that axe which shall sever my neck and the necks of others , or of the conflict ending in a mother ’ s death , which my marriage shall cause , nor of the overthrow of Atreus ’ house .

sections.label: Section 365
sections.translation: But I , for all my frenzy , will so far rise above my frantic fit , that I will prove this city happier far than those Achaeans , who for the sake of one woman and one passion have lost a countless army in hunting Helen .

sections.label: Section 370
sections.original: ὁ δὲ στρατηγὸς ὁ σοφὸς ἐχθίστων ὕπερ
sections.translation: Their captain too , whom men call wise , has lost for what he hated most what most he prized , yielding to his brother for a woman ’ s sake — and she was willing and not taken by force — the joy he had of his own children in his home . For from the day that they landed upon Scamander ’ s strand , their doom began ,

sections.label: Section 375
sections.translation: not for loss of stolen frontier nor yet for fatherland with high towers ; whomever Ares took , those never saw their children again , nor were they shrouded for the tomb by hand of wife , but in a foreign land they lie . At home the case was still the same ;

sections.label: Section 380
sections.original: χῆραί τʼ ἔθνῃσκον , οἳ δʼ ἄπαιδες ἐν δόμοις
sections.translation: wives were dying widows , parents were left childless in their homes , having reared their sons for others , and none is left to make libations of blood upon the ground before their tombs . Truly to such praise as this their army can make an ample claim . It is better to pass by their shame in silence , nor may mine be the Muse

sections.label: Section 385
sections.translation: to tell that evil tale .

sections.label: Section 386
sections.original: Τρῶες δὲ πρῶτον μέν , τὸ κάλλιστον κλέος ,
sections.translation: But the Trojans were dying , first for their fatherland , fairest fame to win ; whomever the sword took , all these found friends to bear their bodies home and were laid to rest in the embrace of their native land ,

sections.label: Section 390
sections.translation: their funeral rites all duly paid by duteous hands . And all such Phrygians as escaped the warrior ’ s death lived always day by day with wife and children by them , joys the Achaeans had left behind . As for Hector and his griefs , hear how the case stands ;

sections.label: Section 395
sections.translation: he is dead and gone , but still his fame remains as bravest of the brave , and this was a result of the Achaeans ’ coming ; for had they remained at home , his worth would have gone unnoticed . And Paris married the daughter of Zeus , whereas , had he never done so , the alliance he made in his family would have been forgotten .

sections.label: Section 400
sections.original: φεύγειν μὲν οὖν χρὴ πόλεμον ὅστις εὖ φρονεῖ ·
sections.translation: Whoever is wise should fly from making war ; but if he come to this , a noble death will crown his city with glory , a coward ’ s end with shame . Therefore , mother , you should not pity your country or my bed , for this my marriage

sections.label: Section 405
sections.original: καὶ σοὶ γάμοισι τοῖς ἐμοῖς διαφθερῶ .
sections.translation: will destroy those whom you and I most hate .

sections.label: Section 406
sections.translation: How sweetly at your own sad lot you smile , chanting a strain , which , in spite of you , may prove you wrong !

sections.label: Section 408
sections.original: εἰ μή σʼ Ἀπόλλων ἐξεβάκχευεν φρένας ,
sections.translation: Had not Apollo turned your wits to maenad revelry , you would not for nothing have sent my chiefs

sections.label: Section 410
sections.translation: with such ominous predictions forth on their way . But , after all , these lofty minds , reputed wise , are nothing better than those that are held as nothing . For that mighty king of all Hellas , dear son of Atreus , has yielded to a passion

sections.label: Section 415
sections.original: μαινάδος ὑπέστη · καὶ πένης μέν εἰμʼ ἐγώ ,
sections.translation: for this mad maiden of all others ; though I am poor enough , yet would I never have chosen such a wife as this . As for you , since your senses are not whole , I give your taunts against Argos and your praise of Troy to the winds to carry away . Follow me now

sections.label: Section 420
sections.original: πρὸς ναῦς , καλὸν νύμφευμα τῷ στρατηλάτῃ .
sections.translation: to the ships to grace the wedding of our chief . And you too follow , whenever the son of Laertes demands your presence , for you will serve a mistress most discreet , as all declare who came to Ilium .

sections.label: Section 424
sections.original: ἦ δεινὸς ὁ λάτρις . τί ποτʼ ἔχουσι τοὔνομα
sections.translation: A clever fellow , this servant ! Why is it heralds hold

sections.label: Section 425
sections.translation: the name they do ? All men unite in hating with one common hate the attendants of kings or governments . You say my mother shall come to the halls of Odysseus ? Where then are Apollo ’ s words , so clear to me in their interpretation , which declare

sections.label: Section 430
sections.original: αὐτοῦ θανεῖσθαι ; ⋯ τἄλλα δʼ οὐκ ὀνειδιῶ .
sections.translation: that she shall die here ? What else remains , I will not taunt her with . Unhappy Odysseus , he does not know the sufferings that await him ; or how these ills I and my Phrygians endure shall one day seem to him precious as gold . For beyond the ten long years spent at Troy he shall drag out other ten and then come to his country all alone . . .

sections.label: Section 435
sections.original: οὗ δὴ στενὸν δίαυλον ᾤκισται πέτρας
sections.translation: where dreadful Charybdis lurks in a narrow channel between the rocks ; past Cyclops the savage shepherd , and Ligurian Circe who turns men to swine ; shipwrecked often upon the salt sea-wave ; longing to eat the lotus , and the sacred cattle of the sun ,

sections.label: Section 440
sections.translation: whose flesh shall utter in the days to come a human voice , bitter to Odysseus . In brief , he shall descend alive to Hades , and , though he shall escape the waters ’ flood , yet shall he find a thousand troubles in his country when he arrives .

sections.label: Section 444
sections.original: ἀλλὰ γὰρ τί τοὺς Ὀδυσσέως ἐξακοντίζω πόνους ;
sections.translation: Enough ! why do I recount the troubles of Odysseus ?

sections.label: Section 445
sections.original: στεῖχʼ ὅπως τάχιστʼ · ἐς Ἅιδου νυμφίῳ γημώμεθα .
sections.translation: Lead on at once , that I may wed my husband for his home in Hades ’ halls . Base you are , and basely shall you be buried , in the dead of night when day is done , you captain of that army of Danaids , who think so proudly of your fortune ! Yes , and the rocky chasm with its flood of wintry waters shall give my corpse cast forth in nakedness to wild beasts to make their meal upon ,

sections.label: Section 450
sections.original: θηρσὶ δώσουσιν δάσασθαι , τὴν Ἀπόλλωνος λάτριν .
sections.translation: near my husband ’ s tomb , I , Apollo ’ s servant . O garlands of that god most dear to me ! farewell , you mystic symbols ! I here resign your feasts , my joy in days gone by . Go , I tear you from my body , that , while yet mine honor is intact , I may give them to the rushing winds to waft to you , my prince of prophecy !

sections.label: Section 455
sections.original: ποῦ σκάφος τὸ τοῦ στρατηγοῦ ; ποῖ ποτʼ ἐμβαίνειν με χρή ;
sections.translation: Where is that general ’ s ship ? Where must I go to take my place there ? Lose no further time in watching for a favoring breeze to fill your sails , doomed as you are to carry from this land one of the three avenging spirits . Fare you well , mother ! dry your tears . O dear country ! my brothers below the earth and my own father ,

sections.label: Section 460
sections.original: οὐ μακρὰν δέξεσθέ μʼ · ἥξω δʼ ἐς νεκροὺς νικηφόρος
sections.translation: it will not be long before you shall welcome me ; victory shall crown my advent among the dead , when I have overthrown the home of our destroyers , the house of the sons of Atreus .

sections.label: Section 462
sections.translation: You guardians of the grey-haired Hecuba , see how your mistress is sinking speechless to the ground ! Take hold of her ! will you let her fall ,

sections.label: Section 465
sections.original: γραῖαν πεσοῦσαν ; αἴρετʼ εἰς ὀρθὸν δέμας .
sections.translation: you worthless slaves ? lift up again , from where it lies , her withered body .

sections.label: Section 466
sections.original: ἐᾶτέ μʼ — οὔτοι φίλα τὰ μὴ φίλʼ , ὦ κόραι —
sections.translation: Leave me , my maidens — unwelcome service does not grow welcome — lying where I fell ; my sufferings now , my troubles past , afflictions yet to come , all claim this lowly posture . Gods of heaven ! small help I find in calling such allies ,

sections.label: Section 470
sections.original: ὅμως δʼ ἔχει τι σχῆμα κικλήσκειν θεούς ,
sections.translation: yet is there something in the form of invoking heaven , whenever we fall on evil days . First I will sing of my former blessings ; so shall I inspire the greater pity for my present woes . Born to royal estate and wedded to a royal lord ,

sections.label: Section 475
sections.translation: I was the mother of a race of gallant sons ; no mere ciphers they , but Phrygia ’ s chiefest pride , children such as no Trojan or Hellenic or barbarian mother ever had to boast . All these have I seen slain by the spear of Hellas ,

sections.label: Section 480
sections.original: τρίχας τʼ ἐτμήθην τάσδε πρὸς τύμβοις νεκρῶν ,
sections.translation: and at their tombs have I shorn off my hair ; with these my eyes I saw their father , Priam , butchered on his own hearth , and my city captured , nor did others bring this bitter news to me . The maidens I brought up

sections.label: Section 485
sections.translation: to see chosen for some marriage high , for strangers have I reared them , and seen them snatched away . Nevermore can I hope to be seen by them , nor shall my eyes behold them ever in the days to come . And last , to crown my misery ,

sections.label: Section 490
sections.translation: I shall be brought to Hellas , a slave in my old age . And there the tasks that least befit the evening of my life will they impose on me , Hector ’ s mother , to watch their gates and keep the keys , or bake their bread , and on the ground instead of my royal bed

sections.label: Section 495
sections.translation: lay down my shrunken limbs , with tattered rags about my wasted frame , a shameful garb for those who once were prosperous . Ah , woe is me ! and this is what I bear and am to bear for one woman ’ s marriage !

sections.label: Section 500
sections.original: ὦ τέκνον , ὦ σύμβακχε Κασάνδρα θεοῖς ,
sections.translation: O my daughter , O Cassandra ! whom gods have summoned to their frenzied train , how cruel the lot that ends your virgin days ! And you , Polyxena ! my child of sorrow , where , oh ! where are you ? None of all the many sons and daughters I have born comes to aid a wretched mother .

sections.label: Section 505
sections.original: τί δῆτά μʼ ὀρθοῦτʼ ; ἐλπίδων ποίων ὕπο ;
sections.translation: Why then raise me up ? What hope is left us ? Guide me , who before trod so daintily the streets of Troy , but now am a slave , to a bed upon the ground , near some rocky ridge , that from there I may cast myself down and perish , after I have wasted my body with weeping .

sections.label: Section 510
sections.original: μηδένα νομίζετʼ εὐτυχεῖν , πρὶν ἂν θάνῃ .
sections.translation: Of all the prosperous crowd , count none a happy man before he die .

sections.label: Section 1.512
sections.translation: Sing me , Muse , a tale of Troy , a funeral dirge in strains unheard as yet , with tears ;

sections.label: Section 1.515
sections.original: νῦν γὰρ μέλος ἐς Τροίαν ἰαχήσω ,
sections.translation: for now I will uplift for Troy a piteous chant , telling how I met my doom and fell a wretched captive to the Argives by reason of a four-footed beast that moved on wheels , when Achaea ’ s sons left at our . gates that horse ,

sections.label: Section 1.520
sections.translation: loud rumbling to the sky , with its trappings of gold and its freight of warriors ; and our people cried out as they stood upon the rocky citadel , Up now , you whose toil is over ,

sections.label: Section 1.525
sections.translation: and drag this sacred image to the shrine of the Zeus-born maiden , goddess of our Ilium ! Forth from his house came every youth and every grey-head too ; and with songs of joy

sections.label: Section 1.530
sections.translation: they took the fatal snare within .

sections.label: Section 1.535
sections.translation: Dardania ’ s ruin , a welcome gift to be to her , the virgin queen of deathless steeds ; and with nooses of cord they dragged it , as it had been a ship ’ s dark hull , to the stone-built

sections.label: Section 1.540
sections.translation: temple of the goddess Pallas , and set it on that floor so soon to drink our country ’ s blood . But , as they labored and made merry , came on the pitchy night ; loud the Libyan flute was sounding ,

sections.label: Section 1.545
sections.translation: and Phrygian songs awoke , while maidens beat the ground with airy foot , uplifting their glad song ; and in the halls a blaze of torchlight shed its flickering shadows

sections.label: Section 1.550
sections.translation: on sleeping eyes .

sections.label: Section 555
sections.translation: when there rang along the town a cry of death which filled the homes of Troy , and babies in terror clung about their mothers ’ skirts ,

sections.label: Section 560
sections.translation: as forth from their ambush came the warrior-band , the handiwork of maiden Pallas . Soon the altars ran with Phrygian blood , and desolation reigned over every bed where young men lay beheaded ,
